epigraph.text:
Every good and perfect gift is from above, coming 
down from the Father of the heavenly lights.
epigraph.quote: The study of mathematics is apt to commence in disappointment.

content.text: {The study of mathematics is apt to commence in disappointment.}{Alfred North Whitehead}

Factoring begins with the simplest technique: finding what all terms 
have in common and factoring it out. This is the reverse of the 
distributive property.

content.title: Greatest Common Factor
content.content:
The **greatest common factor (GCF)** 
of two or more integers is the largest integer that divides all of 
them evenly.

content.text: An efficient method uses prime factorization:
Take the **lowest power** of each common prime: $2^2 \cdot 3 = 12$.

For monomials, find the GCF of the coefficients and the lowest power 
of each common variable.

content.title: GCF of monomials
content.solution:
- GCF of $12$ and $18$: $6$

- For $x$: lowest power is $x^2$

- For $y$: lowest power is $y^2$
GCF $= 6x^2y^2$

content.text:
Factoring out the GCF reverses the distributive property:
\[
 ab + ac = a(b + c)
\]

content.title: Factoring Out the GCF
content.content:
1. Find the GCF of all terms.

2. Divide each term by the GCF.

3. Write as: GCF $\times$ (remaining expression)

4. Check by distributing.

Always check your factoring by multiplying back. The product should 
equal the original expression.

content.text:
Sometimes it's useful to factor out a negative, especially when the 
leading coefficient is negative.
Sometimes the common factor is itself a binomial.

content.title: Common binomial factor
content.solution:
Both terms contain the factor $(x + 3)$:
\[
 x(x + 3) + 5(x + 3) = (x + 3)(x + 5)
\]

marginNotes.text:
How good and pleasant it is when God's 
people live together in unity!

marginNotes.author: Euclid
marginNotes.text:
What is asserted without proof may be dismissed 
without proof.
